addedBlocks:
## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Docket:
  ## Character
  Spec body content rewrite — Windows-side depth. BUS0 architecture and skeleton stubs already in place from pace 1; this pace fills the sub-spec bodies and aligns garrison preconditions.
  
  ## Goal
  Flesh body content of BUSJCW, BUSJIW, BUSJHW; align garrison-w/c preconditions to reference invigilate-windows.
  
  ## Boundary
  - BUSJCW: replace the inherited fenestrate body with the broader caparison-windows scope. Preconditions, steps for SSH trust + WSL stage (always-purge-and-reimport, no skip-if-present) + powercfg sleep/hibernate disable + Tailscale service Set-Service Automatic, post-state assertion via invigilate-windows reference, guarantee, completion.
  - BUSJIW: replace skeleton TODO with posture fact list. Operator-managed registry keys, Tailscale service StartupType, powercfg standby/hibernate state, AoAc override, admin's rbtww-main presence. Read commands (`Get-ItemPropertyValue`, `Get-Service`, `powercfg /a`, `wsl --list`) with expected values. Failure messages discriminate operator-pointer (handbook) vs caparison-pointer.
  - BUSJHW: replace skeleton TODO with operator-manual Windows scope — sshd reachability bootstrap, security-sensitive registry edits (DevicePasswordLessBuildVersion), Tailscale install + Run-Unattended toggle + first auth, netplwiz auto-login.
  - BUSJGW + BUSJGC: precondition lists reference invigilate-windows. Remove the "WSL distribution rbtww-main installed under admin" wording from BUSJGW (caparison-windows owns that posture now).
  - BUS0 already carries the catalog entries and verb definitions for this platform; do not re-edit BUS0 here.
  - No bash code edits.
  
  ## Done when
  - BUSJCW, BUSJIW, BUSJHW bodies render coherently end-to-end against each other.
  - BUSJGW/C preconditions align with invigilate-windows references.
  - bash -n green; tt/buw-st.BukSelfTest.sh green; tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh green.

content.content:
1	# jjezs_paddock ₣A-
2	
3	## Goal
4	
5	Windows test infrastructure for Recipe Bottle. Run RB's container isolation
6	tests on a Windows host with WSL, Cygwin, and PowerShell access paths.
7	Container runtime for release-1 is Docker Desktop for Windows only; the
8	WSL distro `rbtww-main` consumes Docker Desktop's per-distro WSL
9	integration rather than running its own dockerd. Native dockerd inside
10	WSL is named in §Deferred for the shared-kernel-iptables reason.
11	
12	## Current Concept
13	
14	**Jurisdiction** is the BUK feature-area for reaching out to remote nodes
15	deterministically. Logical-only umbrella concept; `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` is
16	its implementation seat (BCG-compliant module housing all three verbs).
17	Neither the umbrella nor the implementation module earns AXLA-entity voicing
18	in BUS0. Architectural spine in BUS0 §Remote Node Access.
19	
20	**Two regimes (BURN, BURP):**
21	
22	- BURN — node-shape (host, OS family). Per-node, git-transported, changes
23	  rarely. Identifier: investiture.
24	- BURP — privileged + workload credentials. Per-station-user. Three fields:
25	  `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER`, `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE`,
26	  `BURP_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE`. Pubkeys derived from privkeys via
27	  `ssh-keygen -y` at use-time; no separate pubkey paths or inline strings.
28	  Identifier: investiture — by construction equal to a registered
29	  investiture. The BURP directory name must match a BURN profile dir;
30	  the 1:1 correspondence is enforced by file-presence check at load time.
31	
32	**BUJB constant:**
33	
34	- `BUJB_workload_user` — project-wide convention name for the workload OS
35	  user that garrison provisions on every node. Single source of truth.
36	  Lives in `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` as a tinder constant.
37	
38	**Three verbs (Caparison, Invigilate, Garrison):**
39	
40	- **Caparison** — admin host posture establishment, per platform
41	  (Windows/macOS/Linux). Nuclear: every invocation purges and re-establishes
42	  the caparison-managed posture from scratch — no skip-if-present logic.
43	  Operator handbook owns security-sensitive edits (UAC-required registry
44	  keys, Tailscale interactive auth, etc.); caparison owns idempotent
45	  admin-shell operations.
46	- **Invigilate** — read-only host posture verification, per platform. Pure
47	  precondition assertions; no mutations. Each posture fact has a read
48	  command, expected value, and failure pointer to either the operator
49	  handbook (for operator-managed posture) or caparison (for
50	  caparison-managed posture). Single source of truth for "correctly-configured"
51	  per platform. Used twice: caparison's post-completion check, garrison's
52	  precondition check.
53	- **Garrison** — workload account provisioning only. Per shell-letter
54	  (b/c/w). First step is invigilate-{platform}; failure surfaces immediately.
55	  Never reads or writes any `sshd_config`; never alters admin
56	  authorized_keys.
57	
58	**Garrison-destructive model:**
59	
60	- Every garrison wipes prior workload state regardless of which investiture
61	  or operator runs it. Node runtime state belongs to whoever last garrisoned.
62	- One workload user per node, ever. No conscript / discharge / withdraw /
63	  vanquish / inventory verbs; garrison subsumes them.
64	- Admin-side host posture is caparison's scope; the security-sensitive
65	  remainder is the per-platform handbook's scope.
66	
67	**Cross-cutting premises:**
68	
69	- `burn_host_singularity` — host of a node lives only in BURN; BURP reaches
70	  it by reference, never duplicates.
71	- `bus_keys_operator_owned` — system never generates or modifies SSH key
72	  material; operator owns all key administration.
73	
74	**Tabtarget colophons (23 total; b/c/w shell support, PowerShell deferred):**
75	
76	- `buw-r[np][lrv]` — BURN/BURP regime config (List/Render/Validate) — 6
77	- `buw-jpC[WML]` — Caparison per platform (W=Windows; M=macOS; L=Linux) — 3
78	- `buw-jpI[WML]` — Invigilate per platform — 3
79	- `buw-jpG[bcw]` — Garrison per shell-letter (b=native bash for Linux/Mac;
80	  c=Cygwin; w=WSL) — 3
81	- `buw-jpS` — Privileged SSH (admin pass-through, all platforms) — 1
82	- `buw-jwk` — Knock (probe workload reachability) — 1
83	- `buw-jwc` — Run command file as workload (shell determined by garrison) — 1
84	- `buw-jws` — Interactive SSH as workload — 1
85	- `buw-hj0` — Handbook jurisdiction top (dispatches to per-platform) — 1
86	- `buw-hj[WML]` — Handbook per platform — 3
87	
88	**Operational seat (`bujb_jurisdiction.sh` + `bujb_cli.sh`):**
89	
90	BCG-compliant module housing caparison, invigilate, and garrison
91	implementations, hardcoding the three shell-letter→`command=` directive
92	mappings (b/c/w) used by garrison on the workload account, the workload
93	privkey destination path on remote per shell-letter, the canonical WSL
94	distribution name (`rbtww-main`), and the Windows OpenSSH `sshd_config`
95	hardening directive set used by caparison-windows. SSH transport mechanics
96	live in code; BURP carries no shell selection.
97	
98	**Caparison-Windows ceremony (admin posture; Windows OpenSSH host):**
99	
100	Three-phase: SSH-trust hardening (former fenestrate scope), then post-trust
101	admin posture, then post-completion verification.
102	
103	Phase 1 (over the password-or-key admin SSH session):
104	
105	1. SSH-as-`BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` with
106	   `PreferredAuthentications=publickey,password`. Steady-state:
107	   `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE` authenticates by key. First-run: ssh prompts
108	   on /dev/tty; operator types the Windows admin password once. Caparison
109	   process never sees the password.
110	2. Idempotently place admin pubkey (derived from `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE`
111	   via `ssh-keygen -y`) in `administrators_authorized_keys` as a bare entry
112	   (no `command=`; uniform across shell-letters). Performed before any
113	   sshd_config change so key auth is available for phase 2's reconnect.
114	3. `icacls` lockdown on `administrators_authorized_keys`.
115	4. Write hardened directives to `sshd_config`: `PubkeyAuthentication yes`,
116	   `PasswordAuthentication no`, `PermitEmptyPasswords no`. Idempotent.
117	5. Verify written state via PowerShell `Get-Content` returning raw bytes,
118	   then bash-side BCG-compliant matching extracting effective directive
119	   values.
120	6. `sshd -t` validates the config (penultimate atomic op of phase 1; a
121	   malformed config aborts caparison before it could brick running sshd).
122	7. `Restart-Service sshd` (final atomic op of phase 1; ssh session
123	   terminates here — bash orchestrator treats the disconnect as the
124	   expected phase boundary).
125	
126	Phase 2 (key auth only):
127	
128	8. Reconnect as `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` with
129	   `PreferredAuthentications=publickey` (no password fallback). Failure
130	   here surfaces a brick — the operator's manual password no longer opens
131	   a session.
132	9. Verify post-restart admin session authenticates by key alone.
133	
134	Phase 3 (post-trust admin posture):
135	
136	10. Stage `rbtww-main` WSL distribution under admin user via `wsl --import`
137	    from Ubuntu-24.04 seed. Always purge-and-reimport; no skip-if-present.
138	11. Apply powercfg sleep/hibernate disable (idempotent).
139	12. Set Tailscale service StartupType Automatic; start service
140	    (idempotent).
141	13. Run invigilate-windows post-completion check; non-zero indicates
142	    caparison's operations did not take or operator-managed posture is
143	    wrong.
144	
145	**Caparison-macOS ceremony (admin posture; macOS host):**
146	
147	Compact: enable Remote Login (`systemsetup -setremotelogin on`), orchestrate
148	ssh-copy-id, apply `pmset` power config, ensure Tailscale service
149	auto-start. Closes with invigilate-macos post-check.
150	
151	**Caparison-Linux ceremony (admin posture; Linux host):**
152	
153	Compact: orchestrate ssh-copy-id, mask sleep/suspend/hibernate targets via
154	systemctl (laptop/desktop hosts only), enable tailscaled. Closes with
155	invigilate-linux post-check.
156	
157	**Invigilate ceremony (per platform; read-only):**
158	
159	Each invigilate-{platform} reads its posture fact list and asserts each
160	fact matches expected. Failure surfaces a diagnostic naming the wrong fact
161	and pointing to either the operator handbook (for operator-managed
162	posture) or caparison (for caparison-managed posture). No mutations, no
163	remediation. Invocation contexts: caparison's post-completion check,
164	garrison's precondition check.
165	
166	**Garrison ceremony (per shell-letter; workload-only):**
167	
168	1. Invigilate-{platform} precondition check; failure surfaces a missing or
169	   stale admin posture before any destructive workload work.
170	2. SSH-as-`BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` with `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE`; key auth
171	   only (no password fallback).
172	3. Destroy existing workload user (if present) via `userdel -r` or
173	   platform equivalent — removes account + home directory; stray files
174	   outside home are workload's own concern, not garrison's.
175	4. Create fresh workload user named `BUJB_workload_user` (unprivileged: no
176	   sudo, no admin group, ssh-only access).
177	5. Place workload pubkey in workload's authorized_keys with the
178	   shell-letter's `command=` directive.
179	6. Copy workload privkey to remote workload account at the path hardcoded
180	   in `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` per shell-letter (so workload can
181	   authenticate outbound to GitHub).
182	7. Validate round-trip — SSH as workload + exec no-op + verify exit 0.
183	
184	## Heat Sequence
185	
186	**Spec foundation:**
187	- spec-bus0-jurisdiction — BUS0 rewrite reflecting current concept
188	- introduce-fenestrate-narrow-garrison — split admin trust + sshd harden
189	  out of garrison into a uniform fenestrate verb (since broadened to
190	  caparison)
191	
192	**Implementation:**
193	- implement-jurisdiction — four-field BURP, `BURC_WORKLOAD_USER`,
194	  `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` module, fenestrate + garrison ceremonies, cleanup
195	  superseded colophons + handbook entries
196	
197	**Caparison/Invigilate restructuring:**
198	- BUS0 vocabulary + skeleton stubs (BUSJPF→BUSJCW; new BUSJC[ML],
199	  BUSJI[WML], BUSJH[WML])
200	- Per-platform caparison and invigilate spec content (Windows depth first,
201	  then macOS/Linux)
202	- Bash alignment via comment annotations (no symbol renames; preserves
203	  grep-archaeology for the upcoming spec/implementation review cycles)
204	
205	**Investigation (independent, runs anytime):**
206	- localhost-fundus-parallel-saturation diagnosis
207	
208	**Practice (Windows host, blocks on implementation):**
209	- WSL distribution install (`rbtww-main` set as default)
210	- Garrison-WSL exercise
211	- Garrison-Cygwin exercise
212	- Docker Desktop WSL integration enable + verify (no native dockerd)
213	
214	## Deferred (named for future return)
215	
216	- **PowerShell garrison + dispatch** — `buw-jpGp` tabtarget + the `p`
217	  shell-letter mapping in `bujb_jurisdiction.sh`. Current heat ships b/c/w
218	  only.
219	- **Adopt** — cross-station-user pubkey install via existing privileged trust.
220	  Today: each station user runs the ceremony fresh from their own machine.
221	- **User regime** — centralized per-user pubkey for shared admin scenarios.
222	- **Separate workload→GitHub keypair** — current shape reuses workload SSH
223	  keypair for outbound; tighten to per-purpose separation if security demands.
224	- **BURP override fields for non-conventional key layouts** — operators with
225	  centralized SSH key storage, non-`.pub`-suffix layouts.
226	- **Localhost garrison/dispatch variants** — deferred; remote nodes only.
227	- **Git normalization on remote** — JJK has `jjx_plant`; BUK doesn't duplicate.
228	- **Cygwin / WSL inner sshd hardening** — caparison covers Windows OpenSSH
229	  only. If c/w garrisons ever require their own sshd to be hardened
230	  programmatically (rather than operator-managed), that is a future
231	  caparison phase or sibling verb.
232	- **Native dockerd inside `rbtww-main`** — Docker Desktop and a per-distro
233	  native daemon cannot safely coexist on the same Windows host: WSL2 distros
234	  share one network namespace, so two daemons fight for kernel iptables/NAT
235	  rules. Release-1 ships Docker Desktop only; Desktop's WSL integration
236	  serves the rbtww-main shells. If isolation needs ever require a native
237	  daemon, it would be operator-managed exclusive lifecycle (one daemon at
238	  a time, never both running concurrently).
239	- **JJK fundus user provisioning under WSL** — fundus is a JJK concept and
240	  currently deferred; the Windows handbook does not include a fundus phase.
241	
242	## Standing Notes
243	
244	- **Caparison-Windows handles first-time admin trust establishment plus the
245	  broader admin posture (WSL distribution staging, sleep/hibernate disable,
246	  Tailscale service auto-start).** SSH-trust phases retain the prior
247	  fenestrate two-phase structure (password fallback on /dev/tty for first
248	  run; key auth thereafter; sshd restart kills the phase-1 session).
249	  Operator-manual Windows scope: install OpenSSH server, enable the
250	  service, set `PasswordAuthentication yes` temporarily, allow the firewall
251	  port, ensure the admin user has a known password, apply
252	  security-sensitive registry edits per BUSJHW
253	  (DevicePasswordLessBuildVersion etc.), install Tailscale + Run-Unattended
254	  + first auth, configure netplwiz auto-login.
255	- **Linux/Mac admin trust enters caparison via ssh-copy-id orchestration.**
256	  Caparison-{linux,macos} wraps the operator-manual ssh-copy-id flow plus
257	  per-platform host posture (sleep masking, Tailscale service). The
258	  operator still does the actual ssh-copy-id one-time setup (or equivalent
259	  pubkey-placement); caparison verifies and applies the rest.
260	- **Two-phase SSH-trust within caparison-windows is structural, not
261	  optional.** `Restart-Service sshd` kills its own ssh session, so any
262	  post-restart verification must happen over a fresh session. Bash
263	  orchestrator treats the post-restart disconnect as the expected phase
264	  boundary, not a failure. The post-trust admin posture phases (WSL stage,
265	  powercfg, Tailscale service) run after the phase-2 reconnect.
266	- **Encrypted (passphrase-protected) privkeys not supported.** `ssh-keygen -y`
267	  prompts interactively; caparison and garrison would hang. Operator
268	  policy: privkeys unencrypted; station-level security covers
269	  privkey-at-rest protection.
270	- **Workload pubkey must be pre-registered with GitHub out-of-band.**
271	  Operator pastes the pubkey into GitHub (deploy key per repo or personal
272	  SSH key) before garrison runs. Garrison cannot validate this; it is a
273	  precondition.
274	- **WSL distribution is hardcoded to `rbtww-main`** in
275	  `bujb_jurisdiction.sh`. Caparison-Windows stages it; garrison-WSL
276	  exports it as a seed for the workload's HKCU\\Lxss. Invigilate-windows
277	  asserts its presence as a posture fact.
278	- **Docker Desktop is the release-1 container runtime on Windows.** WSL
279	  shells reach the daemon via Docker Desktop's per-distro WSL integration
280	  toggle (Settings → Resources → WSL Integration), enabled for `rbtww-main`.
281	  Cygwin and Windows-side shells reach the same daemon via the default
282	  Docker context. No separate dockerd inside the WSL distro; only one
283	  daemon ever runs concurrently.
284	- **Some `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` values may contain spaces** (e.g., Windows
285	  admin user `b hyslop`); audit shell quoting at every variable expansion
286	  and path construction.
287	- Tailscale provides stable transport (Mac sees `rocket` as `100.x.y.z` /
288	  tailnet hostname); not adopted as a BUK dependency.
289	- PowerShell-from-WSL pattern: bash owns control flow, PowerShell is the
290	  Windows syscall layer; one atomic command per call.
291	- `command=` in authorized_keys is OpenSSH-standard (used by gitolite,
292	  GitHub, rsync, borg); shrinks key blast radius rather than expanding it.
293	
294	# jjezs_pace ₢A-AA7
295	
296	## Character
297	Spec body content rewrite — Windows-side depth. BUS0 architecture and skeleton stubs already in place from pace 1; this pace fills the sub-spec bodies and aligns garrison preconditions.
298	
299	## Goal
300	Flesh body content of BUSJCW, BUSJIW, BUSJHW; align garrison-w/c preconditions to reference invigilate-windows.
301	
302	## Boundary
303	- BUSJCW: replace the inherited fenestrate body with the broader caparison-windows scope. Preconditions, steps for SSH trust + WSL stage (always-purge-and-reimport, no skip-if-present) + powercfg sleep/hibernate disable + Tailscale service Set-Service Automatic, post-state assertion via invigilate-windows reference, guarantee, completion.
304	- BUSJIW: replace skeleton TODO with posture fact list. Operator-managed registry keys, Tailscale service StartupType, powercfg standby/hibernate state, AoAc override, admin's rbtww-main presence. Read commands (`Get-ItemPropertyValue`, `Get-Service`, `powercfg /a`, `wsl --list`) with expected values. Failure messages discriminate operator-pointer (handbook) vs caparison-pointer.
305	- BUSJHW: replace skeleton TODO with operator-manual Windows scope — sshd reachability bootstrap, security-sensitive registry edits (DevicePasswordLessBuildVersion), Tailscale install + Run-Unattended toggle + first auth, netplwiz auto-login.
306	- BUSJGW + BUSJGC: precondition lists reference invigilate-windows. Remove the "WSL distribution rbtww-main installed under admin" wording from BUSJGW (caparison-windows owns that posture now).
307	- BUS0 already carries the catalog entries and verb definitions for this platform; do not re-edit BUS0 here.
308	- No bash code edits.
309	
310	## Done when
311	- BUSJCW, BUSJIW, BUSJHW bodies render coherently end-to-end against each other.
312	- BUSJGW/C preconditions align with invigilate-windows references.
313	- bash -n green; tt/buw-st.BukSelfTest.sh green; tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh green.

# jjezs_paddock ₣A-

## Goal

Windows test infrastructure for Recipe Bottle. Run RB's container isolation
tests on a Windows host with WSL, Cygwin, and PowerShell access paths.
Container runtime for release-1 is Docker Desktop for Windows only; the
WSL distro `rbtww-main` consumes Docker Desktop's per-distro WSL
integration rather than running its own dockerd. Native dockerd inside
WSL is named in §Deferred for the shared-kernel-iptables reason.

## Current Concept

**Jurisdiction** is the BUK feature-area for reaching out to remote nodes
deterministically. Logical-only umbrella concept; `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` is
its implementation seat (BCG-compliant module housing all three verbs).
Neither the umbrella nor the implementation module earns AXLA-entity voicing
in BUS0. Architectural spine in BUS0 §Remote Node Access.

**Two regimes (BURN, BURP):**

- BURN — node-shape (host, OS family). Per-node, git-transported, changes
  rarely. Identifier: investiture.
- BURP — privileged + workload credentials. Per-station-user. Three fields:
  `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER`, `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE`,
  `BURP_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE`. Pubkeys derived from privkeys via
  `ssh-keygen -y` at use-time; no separate pubkey paths or inline strings.
  Identifier: investiture — by construction equal to a registered
  investiture. The BURP directory name must match a BURN profile dir;
  the 1:1 correspondence is enforced by file-presence check at load time.

**BUJB constant:**

- `BUJB_workload_user` — project-wide convention name for the workload OS
  user that garrison provisions on every node. Single source of truth.
  Lives in `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` as a tinder constant.

**Three verbs (Caparison, Invigilate, Garrison):**

- **Caparison** — admin host posture establishment, per platform
  (Windows/macOS/Linux). Nuclear: every invocation purges and re-establishes
  the caparison-managed posture from scratch — no skip-if-present logic.
  Operator handbook owns security-sensitive edits (UAC-required registry
  keys, Tailscale interactive auth, etc.); caparison owns idempotent
  admin-shell operations.
- **Invigilate** — read-only host posture verification, per platform. Pure
  precondition assertions; no mutations. Each posture fact has a read
  command, expected value, and failure pointer to either the operator
  handbook (for operator-managed posture) or caparison (for
  caparison-managed posture). Single source of truth for "correctly-configured"
  per platform. Used twice: caparison's post-completion check, garrison's
  precondition check.
- **Garrison** — workload account provisioning only. Per shell-letter
  (b/c/w). First step is invigilate-{platform}; failure surfaces immediately.
  Never reads or writes any `sshd_config`; never alters admin
  authorized_keys.

**Garrison-destructive model:**

- Every garrison wipes prior workload state regardless of which investiture
  or operator runs it. Node runtime state belongs to whoever last garrisoned.
- One workload user per node, ever. No conscript / discharge / withdraw /
  vanquish / inventory verbs; garrison subsumes them.
- Admin-side host posture is caparison's scope; the security-sensitive
  remainder is the per-platform handbook's scope.

**Cross-cutting premises:**

- `burn_host_singularity` — host of a node lives only in BURN; BURP reaches
  it by reference, never duplicates.
- `bus_keys_operator_owned` — system never generates or modifies SSH key
  material; operator owns all key administration.

**Tabtarget colophons (23 total; b/c/w shell support, PowerShell deferred):**

- `buw-r[np][lrv]` — BURN/BURP regime config (List/Render/Validate) — 6
- `buw-jpC[WML]` — Caparison per platform (W=Windows; M=macOS; L=Linux) — 3
- `buw-jpI[WML]` — Invigilate per platform — 3
- `buw-jpG[bcw]` — Garrison per shell-letter (b=native bash for Linux/Mac;
  c=Cygwin; w=WSL) — 3
- `buw-jpS` — Privileged SSH (admin pass-through, all platforms) — 1
- `buw-jwk` — Knock (probe workload reachability) — 1
- `buw-jwc` — Run command file as workload (shell determined by garrison) — 1
- `buw-jws` — Interactive SSH as workload — 1
- `buw-hj0` — Handbook jurisdiction top (dispatches to per-platform) — 1
- `buw-hj[WML]` — Handbook per platform — 3

**Operational seat (`bujb_jurisdiction.sh` + `bujb_cli.sh`):**

BCG-compliant module housing caparison, invigilate, and garrison
implementations, hardcoding the three shell-letter→`command=` directive
mappings (b/c/w) used by garrison on the workload account, the workload
privkey destination path on remote per shell-letter, the canonical WSL
distribution name (`rbtww-main`), and the Windows OpenSSH `sshd_config`
hardening directive set used by caparison-windows. SSH transport mechanics
live in code; BURP carries no shell selection.

**Caparison-Windows ceremony (admin posture; Windows OpenSSH host):**

Three-phase: SSH-trust hardening (former fenestrate scope), then post-trust
admin posture, then post-completion verification.

Phase 1 (over the password-or-key admin SSH session):

1. SSH-as-`BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` with
   `PreferredAuthentications=publickey,password`. Steady-state:
   `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE` authenticates by key. First-run: ssh prompts
   on /dev/tty; operator types the Windows admin password once. Caparison
   process never sees the password.
2. Idempotently place admin pubkey (derived from `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE`
   via `ssh-keygen -y`) in `administrators_authorized_keys` as a bare entry
   (no `command=`; uniform across shell-letters). Performed before any
   sshd_config change so key auth is available for phase 2's reconnect.
3. `icacls` lockdown on `administrators_authorized_keys`.
4. Write hardened directives to `sshd_config`: `PubkeyAuthentication yes`,
   `PasswordAuthentication no`, `PermitEmptyPasswords no`. Idempotent.
5. Verify written state via PowerShell `Get-Content` returning raw bytes,
   then bash-side BCG-compliant matching extracting effective directive
   values.
6. `sshd -t` validates the config (penultimate atomic op of phase 1; a
   malformed config aborts caparison before it could brick running sshd).
7. `Restart-Service sshd` (final atomic op of phase 1; ssh session
   terminates here — bash orchestrator treats the disconnect as the
   expected phase boundary).

Phase 2 (key auth only):

8. Reconnect as `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` with
   `PreferredAuthentications=publickey` (no password fallback). Failure
   here surfaces a brick — the operator's manual password no longer opens
   a session.
9. Verify post-restart admin session authenticates by key alone.

Phase 3 (post-trust admin posture):

10. Stage `rbtww-main` WSL distribution under admin user via `wsl --import`
    from Ubuntu-24.04 seed. Always purge-and-reimport; no skip-if-present.
11. Apply powercfg sleep/hibernate disable (idempotent).
12. Set Tailscale service StartupType Automatic; start service
    (idempotent).
13. Run invigilate-windows post-completion check; non-zero indicates
    caparison's operations did not take or operator-managed posture is
    wrong.

**Caparison-macOS ceremony (admin posture; macOS host):**

Compact: enable Remote Login (`systemsetup -setremotelogin on`), orchestrate
ssh-copy-id, apply `pmset` power config, ensure Tailscale service
auto-start. Closes with invigilate-macos post-check.

**Caparison-Linux ceremony (admin posture; Linux host):**

Compact: orchestrate ssh-copy-id, mask sleep/suspend/hibernate targets via
systemctl (laptop/desktop hosts only), enable tailscaled. Closes with
invigilate-linux post-check.

**Invigilate ceremony (per platform; read-only):**

Each invigilate-{platform} reads its posture fact list and asserts each
fact matches expected. Failure surfaces a diagnostic naming the wrong fact
and pointing to either the operator handbook (for operator-managed
posture) or caparison (for caparison-managed posture). No mutations, no
remediation. Invocation contexts: caparison's post-completion check,
garrison's precondition check.

**Garrison ceremony (per shell-letter; workload-only):**

1. Invigilate-{platform} precondition check; failure surfaces a missing or
   stale admin posture before any destructive workload work.
2. SSH-as-`BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` with `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE`; key auth
   only (no password fallback).
3. Destroy existing workload user (if present) via `userdel -r` or
   platform equivalent — removes account + home directory; stray files
   outside home are workload's own concern, not garrison's.
4. Create fresh workload user named `BUJB_workload_user` (unprivileged: no
   sudo, no admin group, ssh-only access).
5. Place workload pubkey in workload's authorized_keys with the
   shell-letter's `command=` directive.
6. Copy workload privkey to remote workload account at the path hardcoded
   in `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` per shell-letter (so workload can
   authenticate outbound to GitHub).
7. Validate round-trip — SSH as workload + exec no-op + verify exit 0.

## Heat Sequence

**Spec foundation:**
- spec-bus0-jurisdiction — BUS0 rewrite reflecting current concept
- introduce-fenestrate-narrow-garrison — split admin trust + sshd harden
  out of garrison into a uniform fenestrate verb (since broadened to
  caparison)

**Implementation:**
- implement-jurisdiction — four-field BURP, `BURC_WORKLOAD_USER`,
  `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` module, fenestrate + garrison ceremonies, cleanup
  superseded colophons + handbook entries

**Caparison/Invigilate restructuring:**
- BUS0 vocabulary + skeleton stubs (BUSJPF→BUSJCW; new BUSJC[ML],
  BUSJI[WML], BUSJH[WML])
- Per-platform caparison and invigilate spec content (Windows depth first,
  then macOS/Linux)
- Bash alignment via comment annotations (no symbol renames; preserves
  grep-archaeology for the upcoming spec/implementation review cycles)

**Investigation (independent, runs anytime):**
- localhost-fundus-parallel-saturation diagnosis

**Practice (Windows host, blocks on implementation):**
- WSL distribution install (`rbtww-main` set as default)
- Garrison-WSL exercise
- Garrison-Cygwin exercise
- Docker Desktop WSL integration enable + verify (no native dockerd)

## Deferred (named for future return)

- **PowerShell garrison + dispatch** — `buw-jpGp` tabtarget + the `p`
  shell-letter mapping in `bujb_jurisdiction.sh`. Current heat ships b/c/w
  only.
- **Adopt** — cross-station-user pubkey install via existing privileged trust.
  Today: each station user runs the ceremony fresh from their own machine.
- **User regime** — centralized per-user pubkey for shared admin scenarios.
- **Separate workload→GitHub keypair** — current shape reuses workload SSH
  keypair for outbound; tighten to per-purpose separation if security demands.
- **BURP override fields for non-conventional key layouts** — operators with
  centralized SSH key storage, non-`.pub`-suffix layouts.
- **Localhost garrison/dispatch variants** — deferred; remote nodes only.
- **Git normalization on remote** — JJK has `jjx_plant`; BUK doesn't duplicate.
- **Cygwin / WSL inner sshd hardening** — caparison covers Windows OpenSSH
  only. If c/w garrisons ever require their own sshd to be hardened
  programmatically (rather than operator-managed), that is a future
  caparison phase or sibling verb.
- **Native dockerd inside `rbtww-main`** — Docker Desktop and a per-distro
  native daemon cannot safely coexist on the same Windows host: WSL2 distros
  share one network namespace, so two daemons fight for kernel iptables/NAT
  rules. Release-1 ships Docker Desktop only; Desktop's WSL integration
  serves the rbtww-main shells. If isolation needs ever require a native
  daemon, it would be operator-managed exclusive lifecycle (one daemon at
  a time, never both running concurrently).
- **JJK fundus user provisioning under WSL** — fundus is a JJK concept and
  currently deferred; the Windows handbook does not include a fundus phase.

## Standing Notes

- **Caparison-Windows handles first-time admin trust establishment plus the
  broader admin posture (WSL distribution staging, sleep/hibernate disable,
  Tailscale service auto-start).** SSH-trust phases retain the prior
  fenestrate two-phase structure (password fallback on /dev/tty for first
  run; key auth thereafter; sshd restart kills the phase-1 session).
  Operator-manual Windows scope: install OpenSSH server, enable the
  service, set `PasswordAuthentication yes` temporarily, allow the firewall
  port, ensure the admin user has a known password, apply
  security-sensitive registry edits per BUSJHW
  (DevicePasswordLessBuildVersion etc.), install Tailscale + Run-Unattended
  + first auth, configure netplwiz auto-login.
- **Linux/Mac admin trust enters caparison via ssh-copy-id orchestration.**
  Caparison-{linux,macos} wraps the operator-manual ssh-copy-id flow plus
  per-platform host posture (sleep masking, Tailscale service). The
  operator still does the actual ssh-copy-id one-time setup (or equivalent
  pubkey-placement); caparison verifies and applies the rest.
- **Two-phase SSH-trust within caparison-windows is structural, not
  optional.** `Restart-Service sshd` kills its own ssh session, so any
  post-restart verification must happen over a fresh session. Bash
  orchestrator treats the post-restart disconnect as the expected phase
  boundary, not a failure. The post-trust admin posture phases (WSL stage,
  powercfg, Tailscale service) run after the phase-2 reconnect.
- **Encrypted (passphrase-protected) privkeys not supported.** `ssh-keygen -y`
  prompts interactively; caparison and garrison would hang. Operator
  policy: privkeys unencrypted; station-level security covers
  privkey-at-rest protection.
- **Workload pubkey must be pre-registered with GitHub out-of-band.**
  Operator pastes the pubkey into GitHub (deploy key per repo or personal
  SSH key) before garrison runs. Garrison cannot validate this; it is a
  precondition.
- **WSL distribution is hardcoded to `rbtww-main`** in
  `bujb_jurisdiction.sh`. Caparison-Windows stages it; garrison-WSL
  exports it as a seed for the workload's HKCU\\Lxss. Invigilate-windows
  asserts its presence as a posture fact.
- **Docker Desktop is the release-1 container runtime on Windows.** WSL
  shells reach the daemon via Docker Desktop's per-distro WSL integration
  toggle (Settings → Resources → WSL Integration), enabled for `rbtww-main`.
  Cygwin and Windows-side shells reach the same daemon via the default
  Docker context. No separate dockerd inside the WSL distro; only one
  daemon ever runs concurrently.
- **Some `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` values may contain spaces** (e.g., Windows
  admin user `b hyslop`); audit shell quoting at every variable expansion
  and path construction.
- Tailscale provides stable transport (Mac sees `rocket` as `100.x.y.z` /
  tailnet hostname); not adopted as a BUK dependency.
- PowerShell-from-WSL pattern: bash owns control flow, PowerShell is the
  Windows syscall layer; one atomic command per call.
- `command=` in authorized_keys is OpenSSH-standard (used by gitolite,
  GitHub, rsync, borg); shrinks key blast radius rather than expanding it.

# jjezs_pace ₢A-AA7

## Character
Spec body content rewrite — Windows-side depth. BUS0 architecture and skeleton stubs already in place from pace 1; this pace fills the sub-spec bodies and aligns garrison preconditions.

## Goal
Flesh body content of BUSJCW, BUSJIW, BUSJHW; align garrison-w/c preconditions to reference invigilate-windows.

## Boundary
- BUSJCW: replace the inherited fenestrate body with the broader caparison-windows scope. Preconditions, steps for SSH trust + WSL stage (always-purge-and-reimport, no skip-if-present) + powercfg sleep/hibernate disable + Tailscale service Set-Service Automatic, post-state assertion via invigilate-windows reference, guarantee, completion.
- BUSJIW: replace skeleton TODO with posture fact list. Operator-managed registry keys, Tailscale service StartupType, powercfg standby/hibernate state, AoAc override, admin's rbtww-main presence. Read commands (`Get-ItemPropertyValue`, `Get-Service`, `powercfg /a`, `wsl --list`) with expected values. Failure messages discriminate operator-pointer (handbook) vs caparison-pointer.
- BUSJHW: replace skeleton TODO with operator-manual Windows scope — sshd reachability bootstrap, security-sensitive registry edits (DevicePasswordLessBuildVersion), Tailscale install + Run-Unattended toggle + first auth, netplwiz auto-login.
- BUSJGW + BUSJGC: precondition lists reference invigilate-windows. Remove the "WSL distribution rbtww-main installed under admin" wording from BUSJGW (caparison-windows owns that posture now).
- BUS0 already carries the catalog entries and verb definitions for this platform; do not re-edit BUS0 here.
- No bash code edits.

## Done when
- BUSJCW, BUSJIW, BUSJHW bodies render coherently end-to-end against each other.
- BUSJGW/C preconditions align with invigilate-windows references.
- bash -n green; tt/buw-st.BukSelfTest.sh green; tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh green.

**Pace goal:** Flesh body content of BUSJCW (caparison-windows), BUSJIW (invigilate-windows), BUSJHW (handbook-windows) and align BUSJGW/C garrison preconditions to reference invigilate-windows.

**Heat:** `rbk-mvp-3-draft-windows-procedures` (₣A-) — Windows test infrastructure for Recipe Bottle. Triad established at architecture level; this pace is the Windows-side spec-body rewrite that fills the skeleton stubs from pace 1.

**Recent work:** Pace 1 (₢A-AA8) committed BUS0 architecture + 8 skeleton stubs (caparison/invigilate/handbook for W/M/L). BUSJPF→BUSJCW renamed via `git mv` preserving fenestrate's git history; that file still carries the inherited fenestrate body which this pace is to replace.

**Docket (₢A-AA7):**

- **BUSJCW** — replace inherited fenestrate body with broader caparison-windows scope: preconditions, SSH-trust phases + WSL stage (always-purge-and-reimport) + powercfg sleep/hibernate disable + Tailscale Set-Service Automatic, post-state via invigilate-windows reference, guarantee, completion.
- **BUSJIW** — replace skeleton TODO with posture fact list. Registry keys, Tailscale service StartupType, powercfg standby/hibernate, AoAc override, rbtww-main presence. Read commands (`Get-ItemPropertyValue`, `Get-Service`, `powercfg /a`, `wsl --list`) with expected values. Failure messages discriminate operator-pointer vs caparison-pointer.
- **BUSJHW** — replace skeleton TODO with operator-manual scope: sshd reachability bootstrap, security-sensitive registry edits (DevicePasswordLessBuildVersion), Tailscale install + Run-Unattended + first auth, netplwiz auto-login.
- **BUSJGW + BUSJGC** — precondition lists reference invigilate-windows. Remove "WSL distribution rbtww-main installed under admin" from BUSJGW (caparison-windows owns that posture now).
- BUS0 already carries catalog entries + verb definitions; **do not re-edit BUS0**.
- **No bash code edits.**

**Done when:** Three bodies render coherently end-to-end; BUSJGW/C preconditions align; `bash -n` green; `tt/buw-st.BukSelfTest.sh` green; `tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh` green.

**Name assessment:** Silks `caparison-invigilate-windows-content` fits the primary scope (caparison + invigilate windows) but also touches BUSJHW and BUSJG{W,C}. The handbook-windows piece is the operator-manual flip-side of the caparison/invigilate pair, so I'd argue the silks remains coherent — the windows admin posture trio is the cluster being authored. No rename suggested.

**Approach:**
- Read current state of BUSJCW, BUSJIW, BUSJHW, BUSJGW, BUSJGC, plus a sibling completed spec for prose-style anchor (e.g., implementation-era BUSJGB).
- Author BUSJCW first (most substantive, anchors the invigilate references). Author BUSJIW second (its posture fact list is what BUSJCW's post-state assertion references). Author BUSJHW third (operator-manual scope is the complement). Then trim BUSJGW/C preconditions.
- Run gates: `bash -n` on touched bash (none expected), then BukSelfTest, then fast suite.

**Execution strategy:** Sequential opus — document body composition with cross-file coherence requirements. The docket leans on judgment about how invigilate's posture facts surface in caparison's post-state assertion and in garrison preconditions; that's a single-thread design call, not parallel mechanical fill-in.

Proceed?

1	//axhob_operation
2	{bust_caparison_windows}
3	
4	NOTE: Pace-1 skeleton retains the prior fenestrate SSH-trust phases
5	verbatim for git-history continuity; pace 2 rewrites this file with
6	the broader caparison-windows scope (WSL stage, powercfg, Tailscale
7	service auto-start, post-state invigilate-windows check). Below is
8	the inherited fenestrate spec text.
9	
10	NOTE: {busn_caparison} ceremony — Windows OpenSSH only. Establishes
11	admin SSH key trust and hardens `sshd_config` to key-only
12	authentication on the node, leaving it ready for {busn_garrison} to
13	provision the workload account. Two-phase remote-run because
14	`Restart-Service sshd` terminates its own ssh session: phase 1 places
15	admin trust, writes the hardened `sshd_config`, validates it, and
16	restarts sshd; phase 2 reconnects via key auth alone to verify the
17	post-restart state.
18	
19	//axhopt_typed_parameter
20	{burp_investiture}
21	
22	//axhoq_precondition
23	A {burp_regime} profile exists at the given {burp_investiture} and its
24	referenced {burn_regime} profile targets a Windows OpenSSH node.
25	
26	//axhoq_precondition
27	The Windows handbook bootstrap is complete: OpenSSH Server installed,
28	sshd started and set to Automatic, firewall opens the configured SSH
29	port, and `sshd_config` carries `PasswordAuthentication yes` so that
30	fenestrate's phase-1 admin SSH session can fall through to a password
31	prompt on a fresh node.
32	
33	//axhoq_precondition
34	{burp_privileged_user} is a member of the Administrators group with a
35	known password (typed once on phase-1 first run when ssh prompts on
36	/dev/tty; fenestrate itself never sees the password).
37	
38	//axhoq_precondition
39	{burp_privileged_key_file} is unencrypted and exists on the operator's
40	station.
41	
42	//axhos_step
43	. *Phase 1 — open admin SSH*: connect as {burp_privileged_user} with
44	`PreferredAuthentications=publickey,password`. Steady-state:
45	{burp_privileged_key_file} authenticates by key. First run: ssh
46	prompts on /dev/tty for the admin password; operator types it once.
47	
48	//axhos_step
49	. *Phase 1 — place admin trust*: idempotently install the admin pubkey
50	(derived from {burp_privileged_key_file} via `ssh-keygen -y`) in
51	Windows OpenSSH `administrators_authorized_keys` as a bare key entry
52	(no `command=` directive — fenestrate is uniform across shell-letters).
53	Re-runs converge as a no-op when the line is already present. Performed
54	before any sshd_config change so that key auth is available for
55	phase 2's reconnect.
56	
57	//axhos_step
58	. *Phase 1 — lock down administrators_authorized_keys*: apply
59	`icacls` so only SYSTEM and the Administrators group hold ACL
60	entries; Windows OpenSSH refuses to read the file otherwise.
61	Idempotent.
62	
63	//axhos_step
64	. *Phase 1 — write hardened directives*: set `PubkeyAuthentication yes`,
65	`PasswordAuthentication no`, `PermitEmptyPasswords no` in
66	`sshd_config`. Idempotent — already-hardened configs converge to the
67	same byte sequence on re-run.
68	
69	//axhos_step
70	. *Phase 1 — verify written state*: PowerShell `Get-Content` returns
71	the raw `sshd_config` bytes; bash-side BCG-compliant matching extracts
72	each effective directive value and asserts it matches the intended
73	hardened value. Keeps PowerShell out of the parsing business (its
74	error semantics are unreliable).
75	
76	//axhos_step
77	. *Phase 1 — validate sshd_config*: `sshd -t` runs as the penultimate
78	atomic op of phase 1. A malformed config aborts fenestrate before it
79	could brick the running sshd.
80	
81	//axhos_step
82	. *Phase 1 — restart sshd*: `Restart-Service sshd` is the final atomic
83	op of phase 1. The bash orchestrator treats the disconnect immediately
84	after this exec as the expected phase boundary, not a failure.
85	
86	//axhos_step
87	. *Phase 2 — reconnect via key auth*: re-establish admin SSH as
88	{burp_privileged_user} with `PreferredAuthentications=publickey`
89	(no password fallback). Failure here surfaces a brick — the operator's
90	manual password no longer opens a session.
91	
92	//axhos_step
93	. *Phase 2 — verify post-state*: confirm the post-restart admin
94	session authenticates by key and the running sshd serves the hardened
95	configuration.
96	
97	//axhog_guarantee
98	On success the node has admin SSH hardened to key-only with the
99	operator's admin pubkey installed in `administrators_authorized_keys`
100	under correct ACLs, `sshd_config` carries the three hardening
101	directives, and the post-restart admin session has been verified to
102	authenticate by key without password fallback.
103	
104	//axhoc_completion
105	Exit 0 when phase 2 verification succeeds.
106	Non-zero on phase-1 admin SSH failure, pubkey-write failure, ACL
107	lockdown failure, sshd_config write failure, parsed-state mismatch,
108	`sshd -t` failure, or phase-2 key-auth failure.
109

//axhob_operation
{bust_caparison_windows}

NOTE: Pace-1 skeleton retains the prior fenestrate SSH-trust phases
verbatim for git-history continuity; pace 2 rewrites this file with
the broader caparison-windows scope (WSL stage, powercfg, Tailscale
service auto-start, post-state invigilate-windows check). Below is
the inherited fenestrate spec text.

NOTE: {busn_caparison} ceremony — Windows OpenSSH only. Establishes
admin SSH key trust and hardens `sshd_config` to key-only
authentication on the node, leaving it ready for {busn_garrison} to
provision the workload account. Two-phase remote-run because
`Restart-Service sshd` terminates its own ssh session: phase 1 places
admin trust, writes the hardened `sshd_config`, validates it, and
restarts sshd; phase 2 reconnects via key auth alone to verify the
post-restart state.

//axhopt_typed_parameter
{burp_investiture}

//axhoq_precondition
A {burp_regime} profile exists at the given {burp_investiture} and its
referenced {burn_regime} profile targets a Windows OpenSSH node.

//axhoq_precondition
The Windows handbook bootstrap is complete: OpenSSH Server installed,
sshd started and set to Automatic, firewall opens the configured SSH
port, and `sshd_config` carries `PasswordAuthentication yes` so that
fenestrate's phase-1 admin SSH session can fall through to a password
prompt on a fresh node.

//axhoq_precondition
{burp_privileged_user} is a member of the Administrators group with a
known password (typed once on phase-1 first run when ssh prompts on
/dev/tty; fenestrate itself never sees the password).

//axhoq_precondition
{burp_privileged_key_file} is unencrypted and exists on the operator's
station.

//axhos_step
. *Phase 1 — open admin SSH*: connect as {burp_privileged_user} with
`PreferredAuthentications=publickey,password`. Steady-state:
{burp_privileged_key_file} authenticates by key. First run: ssh
prompts on /dev/tty for the admin password; operator types it once.

//axhos_step
. *Phase 1 — place admin trust*: idempotently install the admin pubkey
(derived from {burp_privileged_key_file} via `ssh-keygen -y`) in
Windows OpenSSH `administrators_authorized_keys` as a bare key entry
(no `command=` directive — fenestrate is uniform across shell-letters).
Re-runs converge as a no-op when the line is already present. Performed
before any sshd_config change so that key auth is available for
phase 2's reconnect.

//axhos_step
. *Phase 1 — lock down administrators_authorized_keys*: apply
`icacls` so only SYSTEM and the Administrators group hold ACL
entries; Windows OpenSSH refuses to read the file otherwise.
Idempotent.

//axhos_step
. *Phase 1 — write hardened directives*: set `PubkeyAuthentication yes`,
`PasswordAuthentication no`, `PermitEmptyPasswords no` in
`sshd_config`. Idempotent — already-hardened configs converge to the
same byte sequence on re-run.

//axhos_step
. *Phase 1 — verify written state*: PowerShell `Get-Content` returns
the raw `sshd_config` bytes; bash-side BCG-compliant matching extracts
each effective directive value and asserts it matches the intended
hardened value. Keeps PowerShell out of the parsing business (its
error semantics are unreliable).

//axhos_step
. *Phase 1 — validate sshd_config*: `sshd -t` runs as the penultimate
atomic op of phase 1. A malformed config aborts fenestrate before it
could brick the running sshd.

//axhos_step
. *Phase 1 — restart sshd*: `Restart-Service sshd` is the final atomic
op of phase 1. The bash orchestrator treats the disconnect immediately
after this exec as the expected phase boundary, not a failure.

//axhos_step
. *Phase 2 — reconnect via key auth*: re-establish admin SSH as
{burp_privileged_user} with `PreferredAuthentications=publickey`
(no password fallback). Failure here surfaces a brick — the operator's
manual password no longer opens a session.

//axhos_step
. *Phase 2 — verify post-state*: confirm the post-restart admin
session authenticates by key and the running sshd serves the hardened
configuration.

//axhog_guarantee
On success the node has admin SSH hardened to key-only with the
operator's admin pubkey installed in `administrators_authorized_keys`
under correct ACLs, `sshd_config` carries the three hardening
directives, and the post-restart admin session has been verified to
authenticate by key without password fallback.

//axhoc_completion
Exit 0 when phase 2 verification succeeds.
Non-zero on phase-1 admin SSH failure, pubkey-write failure, ACL
lockdown failure, sshd_config write failure, parsed-state mismatch,
`sshd -t` failure, or phase-2 key-auth failure.

content.content:
1	//axhob_operation
2	{bust_garrison_wsl}
3	
4	NOTE: {busn_garrison} ceremony with WSL bash as the workload shell,
5	for Windows nodes reached via the WSL distribution `rbtww-main`
6	(hardcoded in {bujb_jurisdiction}). On Windows the workload identity
7	inhabits *three namespaces*: a Windows local user (created via
8	`net.exe user /add` plus profile registration in
9	`HKLM\...\ProfileList`) provides the SSH authentication boundary that
10	Windows OpenSSH discovers; the workload user's *own* registered WSL
11	distribution (populated in their `HKCU\Lxss` via `wsl --import` from
12	a seed tarball) provides the distribution registration the workload's
13	session resolves; and a Linux user inside that workload-owned
14	distribution (created via `useradd`) provides the WSL execution
15	context entered by the locked-down `command=` directive. The third
16	namespace is forced by Microsoft's per-user WSL design: distributions
17	register only in the running user's `HKCU\Lxss`, and admin's
18	distributions are invisible to workload sessions.
19	
20	The mechanism is *SSH-as-workload conducted by the privileged
21	orchestrator*. The orchestrator never runs `wsl` as the workload
22	user directly. Instead it places a *bare* workload authorized_keys
23	entry (no `command=`), opens a workload SSH session through that
24	entry — which constitutes a real Windows logon for
25	{bujb_workload_user}, with HKCU mounted — and from inside that
26	session runs `wsl --import` (writing to the workload's HKCU), the
27	inner-WSL `useradd` + `passwd --lock`, and the workload privkey
28	placement. The orchestrator then replaces the bare entry with the
29	locked-down `command=` form and validates the round-trip through
30	that locked form.
31	
32	The Windows workload user is the same shape minted by the c branch
33	({bust_garrison_cygwin}); the workload-owned WSL distribution and
34	its inner Linux user are layered on top so the SSH session lands as
35	the named WSL user (via the locked-down `command=` directive's
36	`wsl.exe --user`) rather than the distribution's default user.
37	
38	Workload-only: garrison destroys any prior workload state across all
39	three namespaces via `zbujb_obliterate_workload` in
40	{bujb_jurisdiction} — the single greppable destroy verb. The
41	covenant scope spans the Windows SAM entry, the `C:\Users\<user>\`
42	Windows profile directory (by convention containing the workload's
43	WSL filesystem root, so purging the profile transitively destroys
44	the workload's WSL distribution registration's on-disk filesystem
45	and the HKCU hive that referenced it), the `C:\cygwin64\home\<user>\`
46	Cygwin home, and the WSL-side Linux user inside admin's `rbtww-main`
47	(vestige from prior garrison shapes; idempotent on absent state).
48	
49	Garrison never reads or writes any `sshd_config` and never modifies
50	admin authorized_keys; admin trust is established beforehand by
51	{busn_caparison} on the Windows OpenSSH server, while sshd hardening
52	inside the WSL distribution remains operator-manual.
53	
54	//axhopt_typed_parameter
55	{burp_investiture}
56	
57	//axhoq_precondition
58	A {burp_regime} profile exists at the given {burp_investiture} and its
59	referenced {burn_regime} profile is reachable.
60	
61	//axhoq_precondition
62	The WSL distribution `rbtww-main` (hardcoded as `BUJB_wsl_distribution`
63	in {bujb_jurisdiction}) is installed on the Windows host *under the
64	admin user* and is the seed source for workload distributions. Admin
65	keeps rbtww-main pristine per handbook discipline; garrison-w exports
66	it to a seed tarball and the workload imports that tarball into its
67	own HKCU. Garrison-w verifies admin's rbtww-main in a pre-flight
68	after admin SSH succeeds; absence surfaces a diagnostic naming the
69	constant and emitting copy-paste operator hints in the
70	`tt/buw-jpS <investiture> '…'` shape rather than letting an opaque
71	`wsl.exe` failure escape downstream.
72	
73	//axhoq_precondition
74	{busn_caparison} has run for this {burp_investiture} so that
75	{burp_privileged_user} can authenticate by key alone using
76	{burp_privileged_key_file} into the Windows OpenSSH host (the admin
77	entry point); the workload SSH session is then opened from that
78	foothold using the workload pubkey just placed by garrison.
79	
80	//axhoq_precondition
81	{burp_workload_key_file} is unencrypted and exists on the operator's
82	station.
83	
84	//axhos_step
85	. *Open admin SSH*: connect as {burp_privileged_user} using
86	{burp_privileged_key_file} into the Windows OpenSSH host; key-auth
87	only. Failure here surfaces a missing or stale admin trust.
88	
89	//axhos_step
90	. *Pre-flight + export seed*: assert admin's `BUJB_wsl_distribution`
91	is installed (`wsl.exe --list --quiet` via the admin session).
92	Absence dies with a diagnostic naming the constant and emitting
93	`tt/buw-jpS` install hints; presence proceeds. Then export admin's
94	rbtww-main to a workload-readable seed tarball under a path inside
95	the workload's profile (admin can write there before workload first
96	logs in, and the workload's session can read it). The tarball is
97	the sole on-disk artifact carrying admin's rbtww-main contents into
98	the workload's identity scope; garrison removes it on success.
99	
100	//axhos_step
101	. *Obliterate workload*: invoke `zbujb_obliterate_workload`, the
102	letter-agnostic destroy helper. On Windows the helper executes a
103	single PowerShell session over admin SSH that purges the Windows
104	SAM entry (`net.exe user /delete`), the Windows profile directory
105	(`C:\Users\<user>\`, including any prior workload-owned WSL
106	filesystem under that path), the Cygwin home
107	(`C:\cygwin64\home\<user>\`), and the WSL-side Linux user inside
108	admin's `rbtww-main` (vestige from prior garrison shapes;
109	idempotent on absent state).
110	
111	//axhos_step
112	. *Create Windows workload account*: provision {bujb_workload_user}
113	as a Windows local user via
114	`net.exe user /add /passwordreq:no /active:yes` (matching the c
115	branch), then register the resulting SID under
116	`HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\ProfileList` so
117	Windows OpenSSH builds a complete logon session for the workload
118	on inbound SSH (Win32-OpenSSH issue #1383). No Linux user is
119	created inside admin's rbtww-main; the workload's Linux user is
120	minted inside the workload's *own* imported distribution in the
121	SSH-as-workload step below.
122	
123	//axhos_step
124	. *Place bare workload trust*: write the workload pubkey (derived
125	via `ssh-keygen -y` from {burp_workload_key_file}) to the
126	Windows-side authorized_keys at
127	`/mnt/c/Users/${BUJB_workload_user}/.ssh/authorized_keys` so Windows
128	OpenSSH discovers it natively. The directory is created with
129	`mkdir -p` and chmod 700; the file is written and chmod 600. No
130	POSIX `chown` is performed — `/mnt/c/...` ownership is meaningless
131	to Windows OpenSSH, which inspects NTFS ACLs; the inherited NTFS
132	ACL from the parent profile directory is admin-owned and read-only
133	for non-admins, satisfying StrictModes. The entry is *bare* — no
134	`command=` directive — because the locked-down `command=` would
135	route through a WSL distribution that has not yet been imported
136	under the workload's HKCU. The bare-then-lockdown pattern is
137	structural to this ceremony; the workload pubkey is derived from
138	{burp_workload_key_file} and owned by the operator, so the brief
139	window in which the bare entry is in place exposes only the
140	operator's own running garrison.
141	
142	//axhos_step
143	. *Initialize workload's WSL via SSH-as-workload*: open an SSH
144	session as {bujb_workload_user} using {burp_workload_key_file}
145	through the just-placed bare authorized_keys entry. This session
146	constitutes a real Windows logon for the workload, with HKCU
147	mounted. Inside that session, perform in order: `wsl.exe --import
148	rbtww-main <workload-wsl-root> <seed-tarball-path>` to register
149	the workload's own rbtww-main in `HKCU\Lxss`, where
150	`<workload-wsl-root>` is a workload-writable directory inside the
151	workload's Windows profile by convention;
152	`wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root useradd
153	--create-home --shell /bin/bash ${BUJB_workload_user}` to mint
154	the inner Linux workload user; `passwd --lock` (root inside the
155	distribution) on that Linux user to enforce SSH-key-only on the
156	inner account; and plant {burp_workload_key_file}'s contents at
157	`/home/${BUJB_workload_user}/.ssh/id_ed25519` (chmod 600) inside
158	the workload's distribution so the Linux session can use it for
159	outbound (e.g., GitHub). On exit, remove the seed tarball from
160	the admin side — the seed has served its purpose.
161	
162	//axhos_step
163	. *Lock down workload trust*: rewrite the workload's authorized_keys
164	file with the locked-down `command=` form, replacing the bare entry
165	placed earlier. The rewrite happens from a workload SSH session
166	because the file is workload-owned (per the icacls grants in step 5)
167	and the workload's session has direct write privilege without
168	ownership gymnastics. The directive content is locked spec text,
169	mirrored at `BUJB_command_w` in {bujb_jurisdiction};
170	`${BUJB_workload_user}` is substituted with the canonical workload
171	user at directive-emit time:
172	+
173	[listing]
174	----
175	command="C:/Windows/System32/wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user ${BUJB_workload_user} --exec /bin/bash -lc \"$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND\"",no-port-forwarding,no-X11-forwarding,no-agent-forwarding
176	----
177	
178	//axhos_step
179	. *Validate round-trip*: SSH as workload, run a no-op command,
180	verify exit 0. Validates the *locked-down* form: the workload
181	session is routed through `command=`, which dispatches to the
182	workload's own imported `rbtww-main` distribution and runs the
183	inner Linux user's shell.
184	
185	//axhog_guarantee
186	On success the node has exactly one workload identity named
187	{bujb_workload_user} mirrored across all three namespaces: the
188	Windows local user (auth boundary, with profile registered in
189	`HKLM\...\ProfileList`), the workload-owned WSL distribution
190	`rbtww-main` (registered in workload's `HKCU\Lxss`), and the
191	WSL Linux user inside that distribution (execution context).
192	
193	//axhoc_completion
194	Exit 0 when the round-trip validation succeeds.
195	Non-zero on admin SSH failure, admin's rbtww-main absence
196	(pre-flight), seed tarball export failure, account-management
197	failure in any namespace, `wsl --import` failure, key-write failure,
198	or workload validation failure.
199

file.content:
//axhob_operation
{bust_garrison_wsl}

NOTE: {busn_garrison} ceremony with WSL bash as the workload shell,
for Windows nodes reached via the WSL distribution `rbtww-main`
(hardcoded in {bujb_jurisdiction}). On Windows the workload identity
inhabits *three namespaces*: a Windows local user (created via
`net.exe user /add` plus profile registration in
`HKLM\...\ProfileList`) provides the SSH authentication boundary that
Windows OpenSSH discovers; the workload user's *own* registered WSL
distribution (populated in their `HKCU\Lxss` via `wsl --import` from
a seed tarball) provides the distribution registration the workload's
session resolves; and a Linux user inside that workload-owned
distribution (created via `useradd`) provides the WSL execution
context entered by the locked-down `command=` directive. The third
namespace is forced by Microsoft's per-user WSL design: distributions
register only in the running user's `HKCU\Lxss`, and admin's
distributions are invisible to workload sessions.

The mechanism is *SSH-as-workload conducted by the privileged
orchestrator*. The orchestrator never runs `wsl` as the workload
user directly. Instead it places a *bare* workload authorized_keys
entry (no `command=`), opens a workload SSH session through that
entry — which constitutes a real Windows logon for
{bujb_workload_user}, with HKCU mounted — and from inside that
session runs `wsl --import` (writing to the workload's HKCU), the
inner-WSL `useradd` + `passwd --lock`, and the workload privkey
placement. The orchestrator then replaces the bare entry with the
locked-down `command=` form and validates the round-trip through
that locked form.

The Windows workload user is the same shape minted by the c branch
({bust_garrison_cygwin}); the workload-owned WSL distribution and
its inner Linux user are layered on top so the SSH session lands as
the named WSL user (via the locked-down `command=` directive's
`wsl.exe --user`) rather than the distribution's default user.

Workload-only: garrison destroys any prior workload state across all
three namespaces via `zbujb_obliterate_workload` in
{bujb_jurisdiction} — the single greppable destroy verb. The
covenant scope spans the Windows SAM entry, the `C:\Users\<user>\`
Windows profile directory (by convention containing the workload's
WSL filesystem root, so purging the profile transitively destroys
the workload's WSL distribution registration's on-disk filesystem
and the HKCU hive that referenced it), the `C:\cygwin64\home\<user>\`
Cygwin home, and the WSL-side Linux user inside admin's `rbtww-main`
(vestige from prior garrison shapes; idempotent on absent state).

Garrison never reads or writes any `sshd_config` and never modifies
admin authorized_keys; admin trust is established beforehand by
{busn_caparison} on the Windows OpenSSH server, while sshd hardening
inside the WSL distribution remains operator-manual.

//axhopt_typed_parameter
{burp_investiture}

//axhoq_precondition
A {burp_regime} profile exists at the given {burp_investiture} and its
referenced {burn_regime} profile is reachable.

//axhoq_precondition
The WSL distribution `rbtww-main` (hardcoded as `BUJB_wsl_distribution`
in {bujb_jurisdiction}) is installed on the Windows host *under the
admin user* and is the seed source for workload distributions. Admin
keeps rbtww-main pristine per handbook discipline; garrison-w exports
it to a seed tarball and the workload imports that tarball into its
own HKCU. Garrison-w verifies admin's rbtww-main in a pre-flight
after admin SSH succeeds; absence surfaces a diagnostic naming the
constant and emitting copy-paste operator hints in the
`tt/buw-jpS <investiture> '…'` shape rather than letting an opaque
`wsl.exe` failure escape downstream.

//axhoq_precondition
{busn_caparison} has run for this {burp_investiture} so that
{burp_privileged_user} can authenticate by key alone using
{burp_privileged_key_file} into the Windows OpenSSH host (the admin
entry point); the workload SSH session is then opened from that
foothold using the workload pubkey just placed by garrison.

//axhoq_precondition
{burp_workload_key_file} is unencrypted and exists on the operator's
station.

//axhos_step
. *Open admin SSH*: connect as {burp_privileged_user} using
{burp_privileged_key_file} into the Windows OpenSSH host; key-auth
only. Failure here surfaces a missing or stale admin trust.

//axhos_step
. *Pre-flight + export seed*: assert admin's `BUJB_wsl_distribution`
is installed (`wsl.exe --list --quiet` via the admin session).
Absence dies with a diagnostic naming the constant and emitting
`tt/buw-jpS` install hints; presence proceeds. Then export admin's
rbtww-main to a workload-readable seed tarball under a path inside
the workload's profile (admin can write there before workload first
logs in, and the workload's session can read it). The tarball is
the sole on-disk artifact carrying admin's rbtww-main contents into
the workload's identity scope; garrison removes it on success.

//axhos_step
. *Obliterate workload*: invoke `zbujb_obliterate_workload`, the
letter-agnostic destroy helper. On Windows the helper executes a
single PowerShell session over admin SSH that purges the Windows
SAM entry (`net.exe user /delete`), the Windows profile directory
(`C:\Users\<user>\`, including any prior workload-owned WSL
filesystem under that path), the Cygwin home
(`C:\cygwin64\home\<user>\`), and the WSL-side Linux user inside
admin's `rbtww-main` (vestige from prior garrison shapes;
idempotent on absent state).

//axhos_step
. *Create Windows workload account*: provision {bujb_workload_user}
as a Windows local user via
`net.exe user /add /passwordreq:no /active:yes` (matching the c
branch), then register the resulting SID under
`HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\ProfileList` so
Windows OpenSSH builds a complete logon session for the workload
on inbound SSH (Win32-OpenSSH issue #1383). No Linux user is
created inside admin's rbtww-main; the workload's Linux user is
minted inside the workload's *own* imported distribution in the
SSH-as-workload step below.

//axhos_step
. *Place bare workload trust*: write the workload pubkey (derived
via `ssh-keygen -y` from {burp_workload_key_file}) to the
Windows-side authorized_keys at
`/mnt/c/Users/${BUJB_workload_user}/.ssh/authorized_keys` so Windows
OpenSSH discovers it natively. The directory is created with
`mkdir -p` and chmod 700; the file is written and chmod 600. No
POSIX `chown` is performed — `/mnt/c/...` ownership is meaningless
to Windows OpenSSH, which inspects NTFS ACLs; the inherited NTFS
ACL from the parent profile directory is admin-owned and read-only
for non-admins, satisfying StrictModes. The entry is *bare* — no
`command=` directive — because the locked-down `command=` would
route through a WSL distribution that has not yet been imported
under the workload's HKCU. The bare-then-lockdown pattern is
structural to this ceremony; the workload pubkey is derived from
{burp_workload_key_file} and owned by the operator, so the brief
window in which the bare entry is in place exposes only the
operator's own running garrison.

//axhos_step
. *Initialize workload's WSL via SSH-as-workload*: open an SSH
session as {bujb_workload_user} using {burp_workload_key_file}
through the just-placed bare authorized_keys entry. This session
constitutes a real Windows logon for the workload, with HKCU
mounted. Inside that session, perform in order: `wsl.exe --import
rbtww-main <workload-wsl-root> <seed-tarball-path>` to register
the workload's own rbtww-main in `HKCU\Lxss`, where
`<workload-wsl-root>` is a workload-writable directory inside the
workload's Windows profile by convention;
`wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root useradd
--create-home --shell /bin/bash ${BUJB_workload_user}` to mint
the inner Linux workload user; `passwd --lock` (root inside the
distribution) on that Linux user to enforce SSH-key-only on the
inner account; and plant {burp_workload_key_file}'s contents at
`/home/${BUJB_workload_user}/.ssh/id_ed25519` (chmod 600) inside
the workload's distribution so the Linux session can use it for
outbound (e.g., GitHub). On exit, remove the seed tarball from
the admin side — the seed has served its purpose.

//axhos_step
. *Lock down workload trust*: rewrite the workload's authorized_keys
file with the locked-down `command=` form, replacing the bare entry
placed earlier. The rewrite happens from a workload SSH session
because the file is workload-owned (per the icacls grants in step 5)
and the workload's session has direct write privilege without
ownership gymnastics. The directive content is locked spec text,
mirrored at `BUJB_command_w` in {bujb_jurisdiction};
`${BUJB_workload_user}` is substituted with the canonical workload
user at directive-emit time:
+
[listing]
----
command="C:/Windows/System32/wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user ${BUJB_workload_user} --exec /bin/bash -lc \"$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND\"",no-port-forwarding,no-X11-forwarding,no-agent-forwarding
----

//axhos_step
. *Validate round-trip*: SSH as workload, run a no-op command,
verify exit 0. Validates the *locked-down* form: the workload
session is routed through `command=`, which dispatches to the
workload's own imported `rbtww-main` distribution and runs the
inner Linux user's shell.

//axhog_guarantee
On success the node has exactly one workload identity named
{bujb_workload_user} mirrored across all three namespaces: the
Windows local user (auth boundary, with profile registered in
`HKLM\...\ProfileList`), the workload-owned WSL distribution
`rbtww-main` (registered in workload's `HKCU\Lxss`), and the
WSL Linux user inside that distribution (execution context).

//axhoc_completion
Exit 0 when the round-trip validation succeeds.
Non-zero on admin SSH failure, admin's rbtww-main absence
(pre-flight), seed tarball export failure, account-management
failure in any namespace, `wsl --import` failure, key-write failure,
or workload validation failure.

{bust_garrison_cygwin}

NOTE: {busn_garrison} ceremony with Cygwin bash as the workload shell,
for Windows nodes reached via Cygwin sshd.
Workload-only: garrison destroys any prior workload account named
{bujb_workload_user} via the letter-agnostic
`zbujb_obliterate_workload` helper in {bujb_jurisdiction} — the
single greppable destroy verb. The covenant scope spans every
namespace a prior garrison of any letter could have written to: the
Windows SAM entry, the `C:\Users\<user>\` Windows profile directory,
the `C:\cygwin64\home\<user>\` Cygwin home, and the WSL-side

on the Windows OpenSSH server, while Cygwin sshd hardening (if used
in addition to Windows OpenSSH) remains operator-manual.

//axhopt_typed_parameter
{burp_investiture}

//axhoq_precondition

//axhoq_precondition

. *Obliterate workload*: invoke `zbujb_obliterate_workload`. On

content.content:
1130	+
1131	{busn_garrison}
1132	authenticates using this key only.
1133	First-time admin trust is established by
1134	{busn_caparison}
1135	per platform: Windows uses
1136	`PreferredAuthentications=publickey,password`
1137	on its phase-1 admin SSH session so that on a fresh node ssh prompts
1138	on /dev/tty for the admin password once; Linux/Mac wrap the
1139	operator-manual `ssh-copy-id` flow. After caparison, all subsequent
1140	admin authentication is by key alone.
1141	
1142	[[burp_workload_key_file]]
1143	//axvr_variable axd_required axtu_path
1144	{burp_workload_key_file}::
1145	Path to the operator-managed SSH private key the workload account uses
1146	to authenticate inbound (from the operator's station) and outbound
1147	(to GitHub or other services).
1148	Garrison copies this private key onto the remote workload account at a
1149	shell-letter-specific path hardcoded in
1150	{bujb_jurisdiction};
1151	the matching public key is derived via `ssh-keygen -y` for
1152	authorized_keys placement on both sides of the trip.
1153	+
1154	The workload pubkey must be pre-registered with any outbound service
1155	(e.g., GitHub deploy keys) out-of-band; garrison cannot validate that
1156	registration.
1157	
1158	=== BURD Regime
1159	
1160	[[burd_prefix]]
1161	//axl_voices axrg_prefix
1162	{burd_prefix}::
1163	Bash Utility Dispatch Runtime
1164	
1165	[[burd_regime]]
1166	//axvr_regime axf_bash axrd_constructed axrd_singleton
1167	{burd_regime}::
1168	Ephemeral runtime state assembled by bud_dispatch.sh during tabtarget execution.
1169	Unlike file-sourced regimes, BURD variables are constructed inline — no assignment file exists.
1170	The dispatch main function IS the kindle: zbud_setup sources configuration and derives computed state, zbud_process_args parses the tabtarget into tokens.
1171	
1172	==== Input Variables
1173	
1174	[[burd_group_input]]
1175	//axl_voices axhrgb_group
1176	{burd_group_input}::
1177	Variables set by the caller before dispatch invocation.
1178
